text: Hey guys, it's Dr. Kajan Singh. Today we are going to watch a small clip of me doing a laparoscopic appendectomy.
It's a short clip but it will give you an idea how do I do appendix surgery laparoscopically.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of laparoscopic ports for the appendectomy, with a 12mm port at the belly button and two 5mm ports on the left side of the abdomen.
The laparoscopic camera provides a view inside the abdominal cavity, revealing the peritoneal cavity's structures and surrounding tissues.
I skipped the part where I was putting the trochars in. Most of the time, or almost always, I use three trochar unless it is difficult or any other problem.
The laparoscopic view shows the cecum and appendix, with visible vessels supplying the appendix and the surrounding appendix mesentery.
The surgeon visually identifies the small bowel as it transitions into the colon within the abdominal cavity.
I usually do my appendix surgeries with an energy device like ligature or harmonic, but in this case I didn't have that available.
The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery of the appendix using conventional methods, revealing the appendix and surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the appendix mesentery, with the blood vessels already separated on one side and the cecum visible on the other.
The surgeon observes the thin and small appendix, noting a possible tumor at its tip. The appendix is visually assessed for division using a stapler.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the appendix vessels, applying two to three rows of staple lines on each side of the vessels.
what company, what brand, and to type. This is the vascular load, which is in this type of the stapler from this company.
The surgeon fires the stapler over the appendix, deploying three rows of staples on each side while a blade cuts through the tissue, leaving a white appearance at the staple line.
The stapler is fired, cutting between the stapled lines and dividing all the vessels in a single action. The stapled lines are visible on either side of the divided tissue.
The surgeon divides the appendix at the junction where it enters the cecum, using a stapler to separate the appendix from the cecal tissue.
The surgeon uses a blue-colored stapler load, commonly used for vascular tissue, which appears thicker than the previously mentioned vascular load.
The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to divide the appendix at its junction with the cecum, demonstrating the stapled line and the separated appendix.
The surgeon places the appendix into a small retrieval bag adjacent to the cecum, preparing for removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon displays a specimen bag with two visible metal prongs on its sides, used for securing the appendix during removal.

The surgeon uses a purse-string suture to secure the appendix within a retrieval bag, closes the bag, and removes it from the surgical field. Any bleeding is addressed by irrigation or placing clips if necessary.
